claim_text: Elevated fasting glucose above 126 mg/dL on two separate tests is diagnostic of type 2 diabetes.
HbA1c of 6.5% or above on two tests confirms diabetes mellitus.
HbA1c of 9.0% indicates well-controlled diabetes.
Metformin is the first-line pharmacological treatment for type 2 diabetes.
A fasting glucose of 400 mg/dL is within normal range.
HbA1c between 5.7% and 6.4% indicates prediabetes.
A fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL is diagnostic of type 2 diabetes.
Insulin resistance is a key feature of type 2 diabetes pathophysiology.
Type 1 diabetes is caused by autoimmune destruction of beta cells.
Diabetic ketoacidosis is a complication of type 1 diabetes.
Hemoglobin below 13 g/dL in males indicates anemia.
Hemoglobin below 12 g/dL in females indicates anemia.
Hemoglobin of 2 g/dL is normal and requires no treatment.
Iron deficiency is the most common cause of anemia worldwide.
Vitamin B12 deficiency can cause megaloblastic anemia.
Ferritin below 11 ng/mL indicates iron deficiency.
Ferritin of 5 ng/mL in females is not clinically significant.
Reticulocyte count is elevated in hemolytic anemia.
Sickle cell disease is an inherited hemoglobin disorder.
Platelet count below 150000 per microliter defines thrombocytopenia.
Platelet count of 600000 is always benign.
WBC above 11000 per microliter may indicate infection or inflammation.
WBC of 18000 suggests no infection.
Neutrophilia is commonly associated with bacterial infection.
Eosinophilia can indicate parasitic infection or allergic disease.
LDL cholesterol above 100 mg/dL increases cardiovascular risk.
LDL cholesterol above 190 mg/dL often indicates familial hypercholesterolemia.
HDL cholesterol below 40 mg/dL in males increases cardiovascular risk.
HDL of 25 mg/dL in males is protective against heart disease.
Total cholesterol above 240 mg/dL is considered high.
Statin therapy reduces cardiovascular mortality in high-risk patients.
Triglycerides above 200 mg/dL are elevated.
Systolic blood pressure above 130 mmHg defines hypertension per AHA 2017.
A single blood pressure reading of 140/90 confirms hypertension.
ACE inhibitors are first-line treatment for hypertension in diabetics.
Hypertension is a major risk factor for stroke.
Troponin elevation indicates myocardial injury.
ST elevation on ECG indicates STEMI requiring urgent intervention.
Aspirin is contraindicated in all cardiac patients.
Beta blockers reduce mortality in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
Creatinine above 1.3 mg/dL in males may indicate impaired kidney function.
Creatinine of 3.5 mg/dL indicates normal kidney function.
GFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m2 for 3 months defines chronic kidney disease.
Proteinuria is an early marker of kidney damage in diabetics.
Hyperkalemia above 5.5 mEq/L requires urgent management.
Potassium of 6.5 mEq/L is safe and requires no intervention.
Sodium below 135 mEq/L defines hyponatremia.
Sodium of 120 mEq/L is within normal electrolyte range.
Dialysis is indicated when GFR falls below 15 mL/min.
Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio above 30 mg/g indicates microalbuminuria.
TSH above 4.5 mIU/L suggests hypothyroidism.
TSH of 8.5 mIU/L is within the normal thyroid range.
TSH below 0.4 mIU/L suggests hyperthyroidism.
Levothyroxine is the standard treatment for hypothyroidism.
Graves disease is an autoimmune cause of hyperthyroidism.
Vitamin D deficiency is defined as serum 25-OH vitamin D below 20 ng/mL.
Vitamin D above 150 ng/mL can cause toxicity.
Calcium above 10.5 mg/dL defines hypercalcemia.
ALT above 56 U/L suggests hepatocellular damage.
ALT of 88 U/L is within normal liver enzyme range.
Bilirubin above 1.2 mg/dL indicates hyperbilirubinemia.
Hepatitis B surface antigen positivity indicates active or chronic infection.
Elevated AST with elevated ALT suggests liver parenchymal disease.
Alcohol consumption is a major cause of liver cirrhosis.
Albumin below 3.5 g/dL suggests malnutrition or chronic liver disease.
PSA above 4 ng/mL may indicate prostate pathology requiring evaluation.
CA-125 elevation is specific to ovarian cancer diagnosis.
CEA is used as a tumor marker for colorectal cancer monitoring.
Mammography can detect breast cancer up to 3 years before clinical presentation.
Pap smear screens for cervical cancer caused by HPV.
Consolidation on chest X-ray is associated with pneumonia.
Ground-glass opacity on CT is a typical finding in COVID-19 pneumonia.
Pneumothorax appears as absent lung markings on chest X-ray.
Pleural effusion causes blunting of costophrenic angle on chest X-ray.
Bamboo spine appearance is pathognomonic of ankylosing spondylitis.
Sunburst periosteal reaction is associated with osteosarcoma.
Free air under diaphragm on X-ray indicates bowel perforation.
Microcalcifications on mammography may indicate early breast malignancy.
Dilated bowel loops with air-fluid levels indicate intestinal obstruction.
Honeycombing on CT is characteristic of pulmonary fibrosis.
CRP above 10 mg/L indicates significant inflammation.
ESR elevation is non-specific but suggests inflammatory process.
ANA positivity is associated with systemic lupus erythematosus.
Rheumatoid factor positivity confirms rheumatoid arthritis diagnosis.
Procalcitonin above 0.5 ng/mL suggests bacterial infection.
HIV RNA viral load below 200 copies/mL indicates viral suppression.
CD4 count below 200 cells/mm3 defines AIDS.
Malaria is diagnosed by peripheral blood smear or RDT.
Antibiotic treatment is indicated for all viral respiratory infections.
Rifampicin is a first-line drug for tuberculosis treatment.
SpO2 below 94% on room air requires oxygen supplementation.
FEV1/FVC ratio below 0.7 confirms obstructive lung disease.
Bronchodilators are the cornerstone of COPD management.
Pulmonary embolism is diagnosed by CT pulmonary angiography.
D-dimer elevation is specific for pulmonary embolism diagnosis.
Asthma is characterized by reversible airway obstruction.
Inhaled corticosteroids are first-line controller therapy for persistent asthma.
Uric acid above 7 mg/dL in males is associated with gout.
Joint space narrowing on X-ray indicates cartilage loss in osteoarthritis.
NSAIDs are contraindicated in all patients with joint pain.
INR above 3.0 in a patient on warfarin indicates supratherapeutic anticoagulation.
PT-INR of 1.0 is elevated and indicates bleeding risk.
Serum potassium below 3.5 mEq/L defines hypokalemia.
Magnesium below 1.7 mg/dL defines hypomagnesemia.
BNP above 100 pg/mL suggests heart failure.
Ejection fraction below 40% defines heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
Digoxin toxicity can cause life-threatening arrhythmias.
QTc prolongation above 500ms increases risk of torsades de pointes.
Warfarin dose should never be adjusted based on INR.
Hypoglycemia is defined as blood glucose below 70 mg/dL.
Severe hypoglycemia requires glucagon administration.
Sulfonylureas can cause hypoglycemia as a side effect.
SGLT2 inhibitors reduce cardiovascular mortality in diabetics.
GLP-1 receptor agonists promote weight loss in obese diabetics.
Microalbuminuria is an early sign of diabetic nephropathy.
Diabetic retinopathy is the leading cause of blindness in working-age adults.
All diabetic patients should have annual eye examination.
Peripheral neuropathy is a common complication of long-standing diabetes.
Foot ulcers in diabetics require urgent assessment to prevent amputation.
Pancreatic beta cell function is preserved in type 2 diabetes.
Serum ferritin is elevated in iron deficiency anemia.
Transferrin saturation below 20% suggests iron deficiency.
Folate deficiency causes microcytic anemia.
Thalassemia is a genetic disorder affecting hemoglobin synthesis.
Bone marrow biopsy is indicated for unexplained pancytopenia.
Streptokinase is used as thrombolytic therapy in STEMI.
Heparin is used for anticoagulation in acute coronary syndrome.
Cardiac biomarkers are normal in stable angina.
Chest pain radiating to left arm is a classic symptom of myocardial infarction.
ECG is normal in all cases of myocardial infarction.
Oxygen therapy should be given to all MI patients regardless of SpO2.
Morphine is routinely recommended for all chest pain in ACS.
Coronary artery disease is the leading cause of death globally.
Exercise stress test is used to evaluate suspected coronary artery disease.
Atrial fibrillation increases risk of stroke fivefold.
CHA2DS2-VASc score guides anticoagulation in atrial fibrillation.
Rate control is preferred over rhythm control in all AF patients.
Stroke is classified as hemorrhagic or ischemic.
tPA is indicated for ischemic stroke within 4.5 hours of symptom onset.
CT head without contrast is the first investigation in suspected stroke.
All strokes are hemorrhagic and contraindicate tPA.
NIHSS score quantifies stroke severity.
Seizure is always caused by epilepsy.
Levetiracetam is a commonly used antiepileptic drug.
Lumbar puncture is contraindicated when there is raised intracranial pressure.
Multiple sclerosis is a demyelinating disease of the CNS.
MRI is the investigation of choice for multiple sclerosis diagnosis.
Parkinson disease is characterized by dopamine deficiency in substantia nigra.
Levodopa is the most effective treatment for motor symptoms of Parkinson disease.
Alzheimer disease is the most common cause of dementia.
GCS score of 15 indicates deep coma.
GCS score below 8 indicates severe brain injury.
Peptic ulcer disease is associated with H. pylori infection.
PPIs are first-line treatment for GERD.
Colonoscopy is recommended every 10 years for colorectal cancer screening from age 45.
Inflammatory bowel disease includes Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis.
Hepatitis C can be cured with direct-acting antiviral agents.
Liver cirrhosis can be reversed with medication in all patients.
Ascites in cirrhosis is managed with sodium restriction and diuretics.
Liver transplantation is the definitive treatment for end-stage liver disease.
Osteoporosis is defined by T-score of -2.5 or below on DEXA scan.
Bisphosphonates reduce fracture risk in osteoporosis.
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation supports bone health.
Osteoporosis only affects women.
Gout attacks are precipitated by hyperuricemia.
Colchicine is used for acute gout attacks.
Allopurinol reduces uric acid production.
Appendicitis typically presents with periumbilical pain migrating to right iliac fossa.
Appendicitis is always managed conservatively without surgery.
Murphy's sign is positive in cholecystitis.
Renal calculi typically present with colicky loin-to-groin pain.
Small renal stones below 5mm often pass spontaneously.
Benign prostatic hyperplasia is treated with alpha blockers as first line.
Eclampsia is characterized by seizures in a hypertensive pregnant woman.
Pre-eclampsia is defined by hypertension and proteinuria after 20 weeks gestation.
Gestational diabetes increases risk of type 2 diabetes later in life.
Folic acid supplementation prevents neural tube defects.
Premature rupture of membranes before 37 weeks requires immediate delivery.
Group B streptococcus screening is recommended at 35-37 weeks gestation.
Childhood vaccination prevents measles mumps and rubella.
MMR vaccine causes autism.
Oral rehydration therapy is first-line treatment for childhood diarrhea.
Zinc supplementation reduces duration of childhood diarrhea.
Exclusive breastfeeding is recommended for the first 6 months of life.
Penicillin allergy prevents use of all antibiotics.
Aminoglycosides are nephrotoxic and ototoxic.
Fluoroquinolones should be avoided in children under 18.
Corticosteroids suppress the immune system.
NSAIDs increase risk of gastrointestinal bleeding.
ACE inhibitors are contraindicated in pregnancy.
Statins are safe to use in pregnancy.
Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy.
Insulin dose should never be adjusted in type 1 diabetics.
Continuous glucose monitoring improves glycemic control in type 1 diabetes.
Depression is associated with increased cardiovascular mortality.
SSRIs are first-line pharmacological treatment for major depression.
Lithium is used for mood stabilization in bipolar disorder.
Schizophrenia is caused exclusively by dopamine excess.
Antipsychotics treat positive symptoms of schizophrenia.
Cognitive behavioral therapy is effective for anxiety disorders.
Elevated ACTH with low cortisol suggests primary adrenal insufficiency.
Cushing syndrome is caused by excess cortisol.
Addison disease presents with hyperpigmentation and hypotension.
Pheochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension.
Growth hormone deficiency in adults causes no clinical symptoms.
